--- SOURCE 1: Fractal antenna ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_antenna

SUMMARY:
A fractal antenna is an antenna that uses a fractal, self-similar design to maximize the effective length, or increase the perimeter

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Fractal antenna

A **fractal antenna** is an [antenna](/wiki/Antenna_(radio) "Antenna (radio)") that uses a [fractal](/wiki/Fractal "Fractal"), [self-similar](/wiki/Self-similarity "Self-similarity") design to maximize the effective length, or increase the perimeter (on inside sections or the outer structure), of material that can receive or transmit [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") within a given total surface area or volume.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/6452553_Vicsek_Fractal_Antenna.png/250px-6452553_Vicsek_Fractal_Antenna.png)

Such fractal antennas are also referred to as multilevel and [space filling curves](/wiki/Space_filling_curves "Space filling curves"), but the key aspect lies in their repetition of a motif over two or more scale sizes,[[3]](#cite_note-r1-3) or "iterations". For this reason, fractal antennas are very compact, multiband or wideband, and have useful applications in cellular telephone and microwave communications.
A fractal antenna's response differs markedly from traditional antenna designs, in that it is capable of operating with good-to-excellent performance at many different frequencies simultaneously. Normally, standard antennas have to be "cut" for the frequency for which they are to be used—and thus the standard antennas only work well at that frequency.

In addition, the fractal nature of the antenna shrinks its size, without the use of any extra components such as [inductors](/wiki/Inductors "Inductors") or [capacitors](/wiki/Capacitors "Capacitors").

## Contents

## Log-periodic antennas

[Log-periodic antennas](/wiki/Log-periodic_antenna "Log-periodic antenna") are arrays invented in 1952 and commonly seen as TV antennas. This was long before [Mandelbrot](/wiki/Benoit_Mandelbrot "Benoit Mandelbrot") coined the word *fractal* in 1975.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Some authors (for instance Cohen)[[5]](#cite_note-5) consider log-periodic antennas to be an early form of fractal antenna due to their infinite self similarity at all scales. However, they have a finite length even in the theoretical limit with an infinite number of elements and therefore do not have a [fractal dimension](/wiki/Fractal_dimension "Fractal dimension") that exceeds their [topological dimension](/wiki/Topological_dimension "Topological dimension") – which is one way of defining fractals. More typically, (for instance Pandey)[[6]](#cite_note-6) authors treat them as a separate but related class of antenna.

## Performance

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Antenna_flat_panel.png/250px-Antenna_flat_panel.png)

Antenna elements (as opposed to antenna arrays, which are usually not included as fractal antennas) made from self-similar shapes were first created by Nathan Cohen[[7]](#cite_note-7) then a professor at [Boston University](/wiki/Boston_University "Boston University"), starting in 1988.[[8]](#cite_note-Cohen-1995-8) Cohen's efforts with a variety of fractal antenna designs were first published in 1995,[[1]](#cite_note-r4-1) which marked the inaugural scientific publication on fractal antennas.

Many fractal element antennas use the fractal structure as a virtual combination of [capacitors](/wiki/Capacitors "Capacitors") and [inductors](/wiki/Inductors "Inductors"). This makes the antenna so that it has many different resonances, which can be chosen and adjusted by choosing the proper fractal design. This complexity arises because the current on the structure has a complex arrangement caused by the inductance and self capacitance. In general, although their effective electrical length is longer, the fractal element antennas are themselves physically smaller, again due to this reactive loading.

Thus, fractal element antennas are shrunken compared to conventional designs and do not need additional components, assuming the structure happens to have the desired resonant input impedance. In general, the [fractal dimension](/wiki/Fractal_dimension "Fractal dimension") of a fractal antenna is a poor predictor of its performance and application. Not all fractal antennas work well for a given application or set of applications. Computer search methods and antenna simulations are commonly used to identify which fractal antenna designs best meet the needs of the application.

Studies during the 2000s showed advantages of the fractal element technology in real-life applications, such as RFID[[9]](#cite_note-9)
and cell phones.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
Fractals have been used commercially in antennas since the 2010s.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
Their advantages are good multiband performance, wide bandwidth, and small area.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
The gain with small size results from constructive interference with multiple current maxima, afforded by the electrically long structure in a small area.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Some researchers have disputed that fractal antennas have superior performance. S.R. Best (2003) observed "that antenna geometry alone, fractal or otherwise, does not uniquely determine the electromagnetic properties of the small antenna".[[14]](#cite_note-14)
Hansen & [Collin](/wiki/Robert_E._Collin "Robert E. Collin") (2011) reviewed many papers on fractal antennas and concluded that they offer no advantage over fat dipoles, loaded dipoles, or simple loops, and that non-fractals are always better.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
[Balanis](/wiki/Constantine_A._Balanis "Constantine A. Balanis") (2011) reported on several fractal antennas and found them equivalent in performance to the electrically small antennas they were compared to.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
Log periodics, a form of fractal antenna, have their electromagnetic characteristics uniquely determined by geometry, via an opening angle.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Rumsey,_V.H_pp.114-118-18)

## Frequency invariance and Maxwell's equations

One different and useful attribute of some fractal element antennas is their self-scaling aspect. In 1957, V.H. Rumsey presented results that angle-defined scaling was one of the underlying requirements to make antennas invariant (have same radiation properties) at a number, or range, of frequencies.[[18]](#cite_note-Rumsey,_V.H_pp.114-118-18) Work by Y. Mushiake in Japan starting in 1948 demonstrated a similar result of [self-complementary antennas](/wiki/Self-complementary_antenna "Self-complementary antenna") being frequency independent.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

It was believed that antennas had to be defined by angles for this to be true, but in 1999 it was discovered that *[self-similarity](/wiki/Self-similarity "Self-similarity") was one of the underlying requirements to make antennas frequency and bandwidth invariant*.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In other words, along with origin symmetry, the underlying requirement for frequency independence is self-similarity. Angle-defined antennas are self-similar, but other self-similar antennas are frequency independent although not angle-defined.

This analysis, based on Maxwell's equations, showed fractal antennas offer a closed-form and unique insight into the invariance properties of Maxwell's equations – a key aspect of electromagnetic phenomena – now known as the Hohlfeld-Cohen-Rumsey (HCR) principle. Mushiake's earlier work on self complementarity was shown to be limited to impedance smoothness, as expected from Babinet's principle, but not frequency invariance.

## Other uses

In addition to their use as antennas, fractals have also found application in other antenna system components, including loads, [counterpoises](/wiki/Counterpoise_(ground_system) "Counterpoise (ground system)"), and ground planes.

Fractal inductors and fractal tuned circuits (fractal resonators) were also discovered and invented simultaneously with fractal element antennas.[[3]](#cite_note-r1-3)[[21]](#cite_note-21) An emerging example of such is in [metamaterials](/wiki/Metamaterial "Metamaterial"). A recent invention demonstrates using close-packed fractal resonators to make the first wideband [metamaterial](/wiki/Metamaterial "Metamaterial") 'invisibility cloak' at microwave frequencies.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Fractal filters (a type of tuned circuit) are another example where the superiority of the fractal approach for smaller size and better rejection has been proven.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

As fractals can be used as counterpoises, loads, ground planes, and filters, all parts that can be integrated with antennas, they are considered parts of some antenna *systems* and thus are discussed in the context of fractal antennas.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*dmbNkD5D-u45r44go_cf0g.png)

# Fractals in Technology: From Nature to Innovation

![Aarib Haider](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*dmbNkD5D-u45r44go_cf0g.png)

--

Listen

Share

In the first two parts of this series, we explored fractals in mathematical theory and nature. But fractals aren’t confined to abstract ideas or the natural world — they also play a vital role in modern technology. Engineers, scientists, and developers have embraced the fractal model to enhance systems across many domains, from computer graphics to telecommunications and even medicine. In this article, we will delve into the application of fractals in technology and explore how these seemingly chaotic patterns are being used to solve some of today’s most complex problems.

## Fractals in Computer Graphics and Animation

One of the most common applications of fractals in technology is in computer graphics. In the world of visual effects, fractals are used to create realistic environments and textures. For instance, remember the incredibly detailed landscapes in films like *Avatar* or video games like *Minecraft*? Much of the intricate, natural beauty you see on-screen — from rocky mountains to dense forests — was generated using fractal algorithms.

Fractals are ideal for generating these environments because of their self-similarity. A mountain range, for example, exhibits detail at every level: from afar, you see the large contours, but as you zoom in, smaller ridges and cliffs appear. Fractal algorithms mimic this process, allowing designers to generate landscapes that look lifelike and complex without requiring manual design of every minute detail.

Even textures like clouds, oceans, or rocky surfaces are often produced with fractal noise. The Perlin noise algorithm, a type of fractal noise, is frequently used in computer graphics to create textures that look natural and not overly uniform.

## Fractals in Signal Compression and Image Processing

Fractals have also found their place in signal compression techniques, particularly for images and video. The concept is simple: because fractals are repetitive, they can efficiently encode large amounts of data. Instead of storing every detail of an image, fractal compression techniques identify patterns and recreate them at different scales, which dramatically reduces file sizes while preserving image quality.

This approach has a wide range of applications. For example, satellite imagery, medical imaging, and video streaming can all benefit from fractal compression. In these areas, where large amounts of visual data must be transmitted or stored, the ability to compress data without significant loss of quality is invaluable.

Fractal image compression works especially well for images of natural scenes — like forests, coastlines, or clouds — because these images are full of the self-similar patterns that fractals excel at replicating. In contrast, more artificial or sharply defined images, such as text or cityscapes, can sometimes pose a challenge for fractal algorithms.

## Fractals in Telecommunications

Fractals are also used in the design of antennas for telecommunications. Traditional antenna designs face limitations as devices become smaller and more complex. However, by applying fractal principles, engineers have created antennas that are both more compact and more efficient.

Fractal antennas have self-similar structures that allow them to operate on multiple frequencies. This multi-band capability makes them ideal for modern wireless communication systems, such as smartphones, Wi-Fi routers, and even satellite communications. A fractal antenna can efficiently transmit and receive signals over a range of frequencies, improving both performance and signal strength.

The compactness of fractal antennas is another key advantage. As devices become smaller, the space for components like antennas shrinks. Fractal designs allow engineers to fit efficient antennas into small spaces without sacrificing performance. This is especially important for devices like wearables and IoT (Internet of Things) gadgets, which require robust connectivity in tiny form factors.

## Fractals in Medicine

The medical field is also tapping into the power of fractals. In particular, fractal analysis is being used to study the complex structures of biological systems, such as the human brain, heart, and lungs. As we discussed in Part 2, many biological systems exhibit fractal behavior, and by understanding these patterns, medical professionals can gain new insights into diseases and treatments.

For example, researchers are using fractal analysis to study the structure of blood vessels. The vascular system follows a fractal pattern as it branches out to deliver oxygen and nutrients throughout the body. Abnormalities in these patterns can indicate the presence of diseases like cancer or heart disease. By analyzing the fractal geometry of blood vessels, doctors can detect irregularities earlier and more accurately.

Fractals also play a role in medical imaging. MRI and CT scans often produce highly detailed images that require advanced algorithms to interpret. Fractal analysis helps break down these images into meaningful patterns, allowing doctors to spot abnormalities that might not be visible through traditional methods.

## Fractals in Data Networks and the Internet

As our world becomes increasingly connected, the demand for fast and reliable data transmission is growing. Fractals have stepped in to improve the efficiency of data networks, particularly in the architecture of data routing and network optimization.

Just as a tree branches out in a fractal pattern, data networks also use a branching structure to distribute information efficiently. Fractal algorithms optimize the way data is routed through networks, ensuring that information flows smoothly even as networks grow larger and more complex. This is particularly important for technologies like cloud computing and distributed systems, where data must be processed and transmitted across vast networks in real-time.

In addition to routing, fractals are also used in data storage. Fractal-based algorithms help compress and organize large data sets, making it easier to store and retrieve information quickly. As data storage demands continue to rise, fractals will play an increasingly important role in keeping our digital infrastructure efficient.

## Fractals in Art and Design

Though fractals have revolutionized technology in many fields, they’ve also found a home in the world of art and design. Digital artists use fractal-generating software to create intricate, otherworldly designs that captivate viewers with their infinite complexity. Fractal art often involves repeating patterns that create mesmerizing visuals, offering a blend of mathematics and creativity.

Designers have also drawn inspiration from fractals to create everything from architectural elements to product designs. The self-similar, scalable nature of fractals allows for versatile design concepts that are not only aesthetically pleasing but also functional.

## Conclusion

From computer graphics to telecommunications, fractals are shaping the future of technology in profound ways. Their self-similar nature makes them ideal for optimizing processes, compressing data, and even understanding the human body. As technology continues to evolve, fractals will undoubtedly remain a cornerstone of innovation, bridging the gap between nature’s complexity and human ingenuity.

In the next part of this series, we’ll dive into the mathematics behind fractals, exploring how simple equations can generate infinitely complex shapes. Stay tuned for Part 4!

The University of Waterloo acknowledges that much of our work takes place on the traditional territory of the Neutral, Anishinaabeg, and Haudenosaunee peoples. Our main campus is situated on the Haldimand Tract, the land granted to the Six Nations that includes six miles on each side of the Grand River. Our active work toward reconciliation takes place across our campuses through research, learning, teaching, and community building, and is co-ordinated within the [Office of Indigenous Relations](https://uwaterloo.ca/indigenous).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Fractal antennas leverage self-similar geometric designs, maximizing surface area and enabling efficient signal reception and transmission across multiple scales.
These antennas operate effectively over a wide range of frequencies, eliminating the need for multiple antennas, thus enhancing performance in compact communication devices.
Fractal antennas improve the functionality and reliability of modern communication devices like smartphones and IoT gadgets, ensuring robust connectivity in limited spaces.
